data.title: Hasbro: Magic Is Working, But I'm Not Spellbound
data.description:
Thesis

Hasbro, Inc. (HAS), the nearly century-old entertainment and toy giant behind brands like Monopoly, Magic: The Gathering, and Transformers, has been on my radar for close to a decade now. I first started covering Hasbro, Inc. in 2016 and gave it a Buy rating. My view was based on the company’s big plan to move beyond just making toys and become a broader entertainment and storytelling company (for example, creating content around its brands).

The call returned ~63% (total return since the rating), but compared with the S&P 500’s (SP500) ~230% gain over the same period, it underperformed. Fast forward to 2024, with digital gaming gains offset by deteriorating traditional toy sales, high debt, and thin revenue growth, and I downgraded to Hold. Recently, the stock has had a volatile few weeks, first dipping after Hasbro confirmed it's walking away from an Indian toy supplier, then snapping back on strong Q1 guidance.

Today, after breaking down what’s actually changed and whether this warrants an upgrade, I’ve decided to keep my neutral rating in place, as Hasbro’s recent strength looks more like execution on expectations than the start of a meaningfully stronger long-term growth story.

Hasbro’s Momentum Is About to Be Tested

  • Revenue: $1.45B
  • Revenue growth: +31.25% Y/Y.

  • EPS: $1.51
  • Adjusted operating profit: $315M
  • Adjusted operating margin: 21.8%.

FY 2025

  • Revenue: $4.7B
  • Revenue growth: +14%
  • Adjusted operating profit: $1.1B
  • Adjusted operating margin: 24.2%
  • Operating cash flow: $893M.

Wizards of the Coast/MAGIC

  • Q4 Wizards revenue: $630M (+86%)
  • FY Wizards revenue: $2.2B (+45%)
  • FY MAGIC revenue growth: ~60%
  • Q4 MAGIC growth: +141%.

Hasbro finished 2025 on a strong note, and its early Q1 2026 results suggest that momentum is continuing. Sales exceeded Wall Street expectations, profits increased, and the company confirmed its full-year targets even after experiencing a hacker attack and supply chain issues. One of the main reasons all of this is working is Magic: The Gathering. The Avatar: The Last Airbender and Final Fantasy card sets were a bonus, and Wizards of the Coast had the best quarter in its history, but again, Magic is carrying much of the company’s success. When a company sells a lot more, profits don’t always rise just as fast, because higher sales can also come with higher costs. However, in this case, Hasbro has been able to sell more premium Magic products, which increase profit margins compared with the small, inexpensive plastic toys it would normally sell.

Tariffs/Supply Chain

  • FY tariff impact: ~$70M
  • 2026 tariff cost estimate: ~$60M
  • 2026 cost savings target: ~$150M.

With respect to tariffs, Hasbro's CFO, Gina Goetter, noted that approximately 60–65% of the company’s total tariff costs for the year came during Q4. Even so, because of efficiencies gained within its supply chain, the company was able to offset the impact of those tariffs rather than have it flow through to the bottom line. The company is moving its manufacturing operations out of China to reduce its tariff exposure and is targeting 30% of toy and game sourcing from China by 2026, as tariffs are expected to worsen, not improve. Good plan. Not easy to pull off.

Which brings us to Aequs. Hasbro is dumping Aequs as a supplier. The official reason given by Hasbro for this move is that it is simply supply chain cleanup and is not related to the hacking incident on March 28. Perhaps so. But switching suppliers during times like these always brings challenges. A new factory partner takes time to get up to speed, and shipment risks are higher than with the current supplier. Plus, it's still unclear whether the original contract with Aequs remains active and what the ongoing discussions regarding it will lead to. This could also introduce additional complications with the switch to the new supplier. The real issue to watch will be whether Hasbro's toy and game shipments in the second quarter of 2026 face delays due to both the fallout from the hacking and the supplier switch happening at the same time.

Q1 2026 Preliminary

  • Revenue: $970M–$985M
  • Revenue growth: +9% to +11% Y/Y
  • Consensus estimate: $909M
  • MAGIC shipments remained on schedule.

Hasbro has handled its public communication on the hacking about as well as could be expected. The company stated that it appeared to be contained, its Q1 financials were unaffected, and Magic card shipments, including the April launch of Secrets of Strixhaven, were still on schedule. Morgan Stanley indicated that Q1 revenue was approximately 10% higher than the same period last year, right in the middle of Hasbro's guidance range, while analyst expectations were for only 3% growth. Operating profit also significantly outperformed forecasts. Once again, it was Wizards of the Coast.

2026 Full-Year Guidance

  • Revenue growth: +3% to +5% constant currency
  • Adjusted operating margin: 24%–25%
  • Adjusted EBITDA: $1.40B–$1.45B
  • Share repurchase authorization: $1B.

Hasbro's management maintained its full-year targets of constant-currency revenue growth, an adjusted operating margin of 24%–25%, and its EBITDA target. That tells us management believes it can see what's coming, at least on the Magic and Disney entertainment side. Overall, Hasbro is expecting to recover some of the lost sales due to the cyberattack in the second half of 2026, with that recovery tied to delayed toy and game shipments being made up. If consumers pull back, it gets dicey. CEO Chris Cocks said on the Q4 earnings call that he’s closely tracking the widening divide between higher- and lower-income households, noting that the two groups are spending in markedly different ways right now.

Hasbro’s Upside Looks Limited From Here

FAST Graphs shows the stock trading at a blended P/E of 16.91x, fairly close in line with its normal P/E of 17.23x, but above the 15.00x fair value ratio. That puts fair value at ~$84, meaning, from this angle, I'm thinking the stock already reflects a lot of the Wizards and digital gaming recovery. Meanwhile, the estimated annual return of 5.14% isn't exactly poor, especially with a 2.94% dividend yield, but it's not compelling enough to ignore that balance sheet. Long-term debt to capital is still pretty high at 72.94%, the credit rating is BBB, and the total EV of $16.18 billion is above the $13.38 billion market cap.

Moving over to Seeking Alpha’s valuation table for more context, it seems to be pointing to the same tension. The forward non-GAAP P/E of 16.49x is only modestly above the sector, but EV/Sales at 3.23x and Price/Sales at 2.72x are more than double sector medians. Price/book is very stretched at 14.13x forward.

I think growth helps justify some of that premium, with forward revenue growth of 8.23%, forward EBIT growth of 15.62%, and forward EPS growth of 16.38%.

But keep in mind, the long-term EPS growth estimate is only 5.90%, below the sector, and levered free cash flow growth is negative on a trailing basis.

Technically, HAS is just above its 10-day and 50-day averages and 13.24% above the 200-day, while RSI at 53.67 signals neither panic nor euphoria.

The forward P/E ratio is expected to fall to about 15x earnings by 2028, which is roughly what many investors would consider fair valuation at today’s price. The stock seems to reflect expectations of strong execution and solid results over a multi-year stretch, with limited tolerance for surprises. Even so, failure to meet anticipated performance in either the Consumer Products sector or the Magic cadence during Q2 will severely undermine this hypothesis.

Final Takeaway

I'm going to hold my shares and recommend the same. HAS isn't a high-flyer, but I like owning this icon in my portfolio. Keep in mind, the majority of my research here is long-term oriented. Only a small slice is based on high-risk quick trades. That said, most of Hasbro’s "Magic-led" earnings momentum appears priced in near current levels. Any income-oriented holders out there can stay involved for the dividend and improving margins and the opportunity to auto-reinvest. As for new buyers kicking the tires, for the most part, you could nibble into this with a small starter position. But I'd wait for a more attractive entry point or stronger operational proof that would improve the setup.

data.title: Realty Income: Improving Real Returns
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Realty Income, Inc. (NYSE:O) has produced modest returns over the last 10 years. In nominal dollars, the share price has averaged $61 while oscillating significantly. Dividends dominate Total Returns. Alpha vs. a real estate index is tiny.

In real dollars the results are more stark; the share price is down almost a third, the dividend is flat, and total returns are only about 3% a year.

These results reflect a buy-and-hold strategy, usually the implicit default assumption, particularly for retirement investors, who often anticipate holding periods of 10 to 20 years.

An alternative strategy that anticipates share price oscillation - i.e. with disciplined entry/exit points - may produce improved results.

Realty Income Overview

The information in this article is primarily drawn from the 2025 Annual Report. The Q4 2025 Earnings Call Transcript (25 February 2026) and the Global Property Conference Transcript (03 March 2026). Unless otherwise stated, the numbers in the Business description section below reflect year-end 2025. Market share numbers are based on Annualized Base Rent (ABR).

This article will generally use a 10-year analysis period. This is long enough to support a strategic assessment while also reflecting the decreasing relevance of more distant historical performance, as the company, the management, and the market environment have materially changed.

There are some minor differences in historical data between Seeking Alpha, YCharts, and other sources, but these discrepancies do not appear to be material to the analysis.

In a March 2025 article, Realty Income: Unjustified Enthusiasm, I wrote that the previous decade's data "suggests that while O will probably provide modest gains, attractive returns will demand opportunistic, rather than automatic, investment."

We will look at O's individual and relative performance in nominal dollars, performance in real terms, examine an alternative to buy and hold, and discuss several issues and risks to consider in assessing O's potential future performance.

The Business

O is the 7th largest REIT by market cap (per Seeking Alpha), with 16 thousand properties. Marketed as The Monthly Dividend Company®, with a declared objective to deliver dependable monthly dividends, O enjoys iconic status with many retail investors.

While historically and still primarily focused on owning standalone retail properties in the United States, rented under net lease terms, it has diversified by property type, geography, and ownership structure.

Industrial (15%) and gambling (3%) properties have been added.

About 19% of annual rent comes from 8 countries in Europe (primarily the U.K.), and about 60% of investment in 2025 was directed to Europe. In January 2026, the footprint was extended to Mexico.

Expanding beyond traditional equity ownership, by the end of 2025, Realty Income held about $3.1 billion in loans and preferred equity interest. Private capital funds (where Realty Income holds a less than 100% stake and earns a management fee) have been introduced. Major deals in 2026 include an investment by the Singapore sovereign wealth fund, GIC, to fund a joint venture fund to develop build-to-suit industrial properties in Mexico, and a $1 billion investment by Apollo Global Management (APO) to acquire a 49% interest in a joint venture portfolio of 500 current net lease properties, to be managed by O.

In 2025, Realty Income evaluated about $120 billion in potential investments (55% US, 45% Europe) and actually invested $6 billion, i.e., a 5% rate for buy vs. assess. They believe their organization can support a $10-12 billion per year buy rate.

About 32% of annual revenue comes from investment-grade clients, and about 35% of revenue comes from their 20 largest clients. Grocery (11%) and convenience stores (10%) are the two largest concentrations of revenue by industry.

In 2025, AFFO per share was $4.28, rent recapture was 103.9%, $6.2 billion was invested, 7.3% initial cash yield, 425 properties were sold for $744 million. The portfolio had contractual ABR increases of about 1.5% CAGR. O had a 5.4x net debt to adjusted EBITDA, an annualized run rate of $900 million in free cash flow, and 3.2% cash G&A. Headcount increased 16% in 2025, to 554.

For 2026, O estimates AFFO per share of $4.38 to $4.42 (up 2.8%), and $8 billion in investments (about 3:1 public vs. private capital), with dispositions similar to 2025. O expects to generate approximately $10 million of base management fees from private funds (with about 70% margins).

O enjoys an A- credit rating from S&P.

Realty Income Performance

We will begin by looking at performance over the past decade, assuming a buy-and-hold strategy.

Share Price

The two charts below show the nominal share price. The average share price for the decade is about $61, with multiple excursions above and below this average value. In my view, these two charts alone should make investors cautious about automatic investment strategies.

Currently the share price is up about 10% for the decade, but that can vary significantly, depending on the specific period selected for analysis.

By eye, most of the variance falls within +- 15% of the average, i.e., in a range of about $52-$70.

Dividends

Dividends are an important part of O's return and are paid monthly. The nominal dividend is up 40% over 10 years.

As might be expected given the roughly flat stock price, the resulting yield has grown over the decade by about 31%.

The average yield is 4.37% over the decade (shown in the chart below) but has been increasing in recent years; 4.58% over five years, 4.79% over three years, and 5.07% over one year.

Total Return

Most investors value Total Return, reflecting the impact of both share price and dividends. The chart below includes both Total Return and Share Price, which highlights that the bulk – roughly 85% – of the Total Return over the last decade comes from dividends rather than share price appreciation. This is particularly evident in the most recent years.

Real Returns

Thus far, we have been considering nominal returns, i.e., ignoring inflation. However, investors buy future groceries and gasoline with real dollars.

In nominal terms, over the decade the share price has increased about 10%, the dividend about 40%, and the Total Return has been about 76%.

Inflation over the last 10 years has been about 39%, per the inflation calculator provided by the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics.

Thus, in real terms, considering inflation, over the last decade the real share price is down about 29%, the dividend is up about 1%, and the total return is about 37%.

Note that this assumes reinvestment of the dividend and no losses to taxes. Under that assumption, one might assess O as a reasonable store of value. However, if an investor is spending the dividends on monthly bills, they would now have a significant real capital loss and a dividend payout that has kept up with inflation.

An investor might ask if there are better options. To try to answer that question, we will first consider how O looks in comparison to some other investments and then take a look at an alternative to a buy-and-hold strategy.

Realty Income's Relative Performance

Let's first take a look at O's Total Return performance vs. other large REITs. The comparison group here are the six REITs with larger market caps, plus the Vanguard Real Estate Index ETF (VNQ), which provides a broad real estate market benchmark. (Note we are back to nominal dollars here).

All of these names are exposed to the same fluctuations in general economic conditions, such as interest rates, but may reflect sector-specific issues, e.g., supply and demand in the sector. In hindsight, O was not the best choice for this decade.

The correlation between O and VNQ is very notable and is more clearly evident in the plot below. O demonstrated almost no Alpha advantage vs. the index, which frankly surprised me.

O is still primarily a triple net retail REIT, although it has a material industrial component (15% of ABR) and is branching out into other sectors. If we look at O and three other (and significantly smaller) REITs focused on the retail triple net sector, we get the chart below. O doesn't win this intra-sector contest.

Finally, we recall many investors' comments to the effect that O is now a "bond equivalent". We can look at some data below, also keeping in mind that if we adopt an alternative to buy and hold, we will also need a place to park the money during periods when we are not holding O.

We look below at two well-regarded bond funds, a high-yield corporate bond fund and a short-term corporate bond fund, but there are many others.

The Total Return from the high-yield fund is similar to O over the decade, but with much less variation. The short-term bond is, as one might expect, even steadier but provides less than half the Total Return.

If we look at the yield differences, shown in the two charts below, the high-yield bond fund averages about 1.1% higher yield than O, and the short-term bond about 1.7% less than O.

If we focus in on just the last three years (which omits the extreme COVID-era variations and ultra-low rate period), we see the yield from O falling between the yields from the bond funds. A mix of the two bond funds would have generally matched O's yield with more stability.

Valuation

Seeking Alpha reports the Wall Street consensus view is that O is slightly below fair value today, i.e., about 8% below the average 12-month forward estimates of $68, with a range of $62 to $75. The analyst's average rating has been fairly steadily declining for about 2.5 years, from 3.9 to 3.4, with 16 of 24 analysts currently rating it a Hold.

One may view the tone and focus of analyst's questions in the last earnings call as very politely probing O's plans and ability to grow materially. From one analyst:

I wanted to go back to your comments regarding the other investment avenues maturing and getting back to a more historical level of growth in 3 to 5 years, especially considering many investors are not necessarily looking for a long-term wait-and-see story.

Seeking Alpha's summary Valuation rating is C+.

In my view, O is probably about fairly valued at its current price. It's notable how close this is to the $61 average for the last decade.

Alternative Strategy: Disciplined Buy/Sell

Let's look at an alternative to the default buy-and-hold strategy, specifically a disciplined buy/sell strategy based on nominal share price.

Nominal Share Price - Buy at $55, Sell at $65

The first step is to look at the data on O's price variance. Initial inspection of share price plots may be misleading, as the curves are both sometime smoothed and difficult to read in detail. Fortunately, Seeking Alpha offers share price data in tabular format, with several temporal granularities. I pulled the monthly high and low data from January 2016 to February 2026 and plotted it in the graph below.

As we noted in the earlier Y-Chart of price data, the decade average price is about $61. The average intra-monthly high-low price delta is $4.56. By inspection of the monthly data points in the table and graphic, a "Buy at $55, Sell at $65" strategy looks reasonable. Note that this strategy generates an 18% capital gain per buy/sell cycle.

Reviewing the tabular data makes it easy to identify the timing of the buy and sell events, which are summarized below. I've also added the duration of the holding periods with this model.

This strategy would have signaled 5 Buy-Sell cycles over the past 10 years, leaving the investor holding O about half the time and an alternative asset half the time. With this model, an investor would currently be out of O.

The 5 buy/sell cycles at 18% would have yielded a 91% capital gain. Assuming the cash was put into a mix of bond funds when not holding O, one might conservatively estimate an additional 40% return from dividends. That would give a rough back-of-the-envelope Total Return for the decade of about 130%, approaching twice the 76% actually observed.

This nominal price point strategy might be refined. One drawback is that it ignores the influence of inflation. For instance, if we started with buy/sell points at $55/$65 and adjusted for CPI over the last decade, we now get points at $76/$90. Limiting the adjustment to $1 a year (i.e., about 1.5%) would generate current buy/sell points at $65/$75.

Basing buy/sell points on dividend yield might also offer advantages.

Some Observations

I'd like to make some observations here based on the data we looked at earlier and two more general observations relevant to O's plans to increase their growth rate.

Performance

  • O's nominal share price has on average been remarkably flat, albeit with +-15% excursions.
  • O significantly underperformed five of six other larger REITs. It also underperformed significantly smaller REITs in the retail triple net sector.
  • O's Total Return was highly correlated with, and only slightly exceeded, the real estate index.
  • O roughly matched high-yield bond fund performance, due to weak O share price performance.
  • High-yield and/or short-term corporate bond funds might reasonably serve as a cash-holding mechanism if an investor is temporarily out of O.
  • The dividend penalty for being out of O is probably 2% or less and could be quite small with a split between high-yield and corporate bond funds.
  • An alternative strategy, disciplined buy/sell at nominal share price points, would have produced almost twice the Total Return delivered by buy and hold.

European Expansion Carries Risks

GDP per person in almost every country in Europe, including the UK, is lower than Mississippi, the poorest state in the US, according to this analysis. Projections by the International Monetary Fund suggest GDP growth for the EU will be lower than for the US.

Among other factors, energy costs are substantially higher in Europe (~2x to 4x US prices), particularly for industrial users.

Management Fees May Not Be Material

O has talked about the advantages of the Spread + Fee model, i.e., earning a fee by managing an investment 80% funded with external capital. In the last earnings call, Jonathan Pong, Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer, responded to a question:

let's just assume, for round numbers, we're getting about 1 point from the 80% of equity we're managing on someone else's behalf

They expect to earn about $10 million in such fees in 2026, with a roughly 70% margin after expenses. $10 billion under management would earn $100 million before costs. Assume costs are insignificant at that level. Spread across the current 932 million shares, that's about 11 cents a share. Against a $4.40 FFO/share baseline, that's a boost of about 2.5%. Not nothing, but not much.

Risks to Thesis

There are risks to the "buy at $55, sell at $65" thesis. I've listed a few below and suggested some mitigation actions.

1. O's average price may move permanently and materially higher, leaving the investor who sold at $65 with no opportunity to reenter at $55, or even $65.

This risk is mitigated to a degree by the temporary holding investment. There are also other fish in the sea.

2. With inflation at even 2-3%, the $55/$65 limits may become increasingly stale.

A reasonable mitigation option is to increase the limits by a dollar or two each year, i.e., about 1.5-3.0%.

3. The suggested $55/$65 limits might not be the best choices.

This is almost certainly true, and each investor who considers this strategy will have to decide that for themselves.

4. This strategy looks like market timing, the bane of many investors.

Yes. The willingness (and ability) to wait for the trade, perhaps for a couple of years, mitigates this at least to a degree. Patience is among the very few advantages available to individual investors. It also demands that investors pay some attention to O's price, which some may be unwilling or unable to do.

Investor Takeaway

A buy-and-hold strategy for Realty Income has not performed well over the last decade compared to other large REITs or other retail triple-net sector REITs. The current share price is still near the decade average of $61.

In real dollars, the Total Return over the decade has been modest; the share price is down by a third, and the dividend is flat.

An alternative strategy, disciplined buy/sell based on $55/$65 share price points, would have delivered nearly twice the Total Returns vs. a buy-and-hold strategy. Investors may wish to consider this or other alternatives.

Personally, I plan to try this strategy. I now have a limit order in place to sell 50% of my O shares at $65. My intent is to rebuy those shares around $55.

data.title: Harley-Davidson: Possibly The Next Big Short Squeeze
data.description:
Harley-Davidson, Inc. (HOG) is an iconic motorcycle manufacturer facing tariff impact, secular decline, an aging customer base, and declining market shares. The company's stock continues to be heavily shorted despite being undervalued—even for a declining company. We will now revisit HOG ahead of their Q1 earnings release and strategic plan presentation on May 5th. This article is an update to our previous article on the company.

Since our article, the stock has performed well, with a total return of over 17% in under 3 months, compared to an S&P 500 change of just under 3% over the same period. This move was supported by some notable insider purchases. However, the company is still trading well below our valuation target, and we still haven't seen large-scale short-covering.

Secular Decline

Looking back at the beautiful illustration of Harley-Davidson Unit Registration from a 2025 article by fellow Seeking Alpha contributor Caffital Research, the secular decline since the highs in the 2010s becomes clear. I also recommend checking out their latest article on HOG, providing valuable insights.

Based on updated data from the company's most recent 10-K, the trend continues with both registrations and market share declining compared to 2024.

The latest registration data shows a grim picture: the company has a declining market share in a declining market—both overall industry new motorcycle registrations declined, and their market share declined as well.

We covered the decline and financial metrics over the years in depth in our last article. The overall big picture remains unchanged: the company is still in secular decline. The decline is even accelerating based on the latest 10-K. In this article, however, we want to focus on the company's recently presented strategic plan and the continued short-selling of the stock.

Short-Selling

As extensively shown, Harley-Davidson is very visibly in long-term decline. This makes the company an extremely popular short-selling target for institutions and retail investors alike. Historically, the company has been a very profitable short target with a 5-year decline of over 52%.

The extreme pessimism surrounding the stock also becomes clear when looking at the comments on our previous article, broader social media negativity, or press coverage. Besides our “Strong Buy” rating early this year, there also hasn't been a single “Buy” or “Strong Buy” article on Seeking Alpha since June 2024. Mid-2024 is also around the last time Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating rated the stock a “Buy”; since then, it has been rated a “Hold” or “Sell” only.

HOG isn't a battleground stock—it's been a consensus short for years now.

While short-sellers definitely have been “right” so far, it looks like they have overdone it. As touched upon in our last article, we believe short-sellers have created somewhat of a self-fulfilling prophecy. This became obvious following the price increase from the strategic partnership with KKR and PIMCO in July 2025.

The relationship between stock price and short interest becomes even more evident when plotting share price snapshots with short interest snapshots over time.

There is a clear correlation: the share price is falling when net-short interest increases, and it's rising when net-short interest falls.

The most recent moves since we published our articles reveal a very interesting dynamic:

  1. Shortly after publishing our article at the end of January, the price fell to a bottom of under $18 in mid-March. In the same timeframe, net short interest increased from just 15.9 million shares on 15th January to over 18.3 million by mid-March. So short sellers added almost 2.5 million in net shorts in just around 2 months, correlating with a decline in price from just under $20 to under $18.
  2. Following the bottom in mid-March, the share price started recovering to almost $23 by mid-April. This correlates with a reduction in net short interest from over 18.3 million shares to around 16 million shares. So short sellers covered around 2.3 million in net shorts in a month, correlating with a rise in price from under $18 to just under $23.
  3. Cumulatively, shorts in this timeframe have been unprofitable. Net shorts were added at prices between around $18 and $20 and were covered at prices between $20 and $23. It also shows how covering 2.3m short shares led to a much stronger move than shorting 2.5m shares in the first place.

As the company is trading close to net financial assets and below any reasonable fair value estimates, short-sellers are fundamentally wrong. The relationship has now changed from fundamental to technical:

Short sellers stopped profiting from the declining stock price of a fundamentally weak company, and instead, they now start to cause technical weakness in the stock of a fundamentally undervalued company.

Once short sellers stop profiting from the decline and instead start causing it, they go from being structurally profitable to unprofitable. This is beautifully shown in the recent moves explained above.

Of course, this cannot continue indefinitely. At some point, short-sellers will have to cover their positions, whether to take profits or to cut losses. We don't think they'll be able to cover short interest at prices anywhere close to the current stock price, as shown in recent weeks, when just a small share of shorts were covered.

Additionally, the company has been aggressively buying back shares, putting further pressure on short-sellers. As a result of aggressive repurchases, short interest as a percentage of share float has been rising rapidly.

As can be seen in the charts above, short interest measured in shares is much lower than it was during the massive COVID-19 dip in 2020. When looking at short interest as a share of the float, however, short interest is close to its highest levels, at a massive 14.5% short interest as of 15th April 2026, around as high as the 15% peak-Covid short interest the stock had in 2020.

The pressure on short-sellers to cover their positions increases with time. We believe this all could either lead to a steady re-rating of the stock closer to our valuation target or even a violent short squeeze, causing the stock price to go even past our target.

In our opinion, the upcoming earnings release and strategic plan presentation could become a catalyst for an upside move. Following the release, we could also see further insider buying and share repurchases, putting even more pressure on short-sellers.

Valuation

The short-selling thesis for this company is extremely speculative and shouldn't be depended upon. As we believe the company is highly undervalued on a fundamental basis, we are confident even when ignoring short-selling theories.

Our valuation will become more refined once the company shares its strategic plan going forward. For now, however, we will stick to our valuation method from our last article.

As our last valuation, we will discount this adjusted value by 20%, resulting in an adjusted EV of around $900 million as of writing. Assuming 2026E FCF of around $300m at a 5x multiple, our target EV for the company is $1.5b. Adding the adjusted tangible book value of $1.6b leaves us with a $3.1b market cap for the company at our valuation target, or just under $30 per share at current shares outstanding. We will update our valuation based on the strategic plan.

Based on the extremely high short interest and continued repurchases at very attractive prices, we believe that the short-term potential for the stock could be even higher based on its short-squeeze potential.

The fact that the company is expected to continue to generate positive cash flow while already sitting on a big pile of cash emphasizes the current undervaluation and provides good context as to why we think our valuation is conservative.

Outlook

In the presentation, the company is planning to hold on 5th May, primarily because their strategic plan is important for investors. However, the date will also be used to release their Q1 2026 financial data.

Since the company, in their last earnings presentation, guided for a stabilization between sales and wholesale shipments, an important metric will be whether and how big any difference between those two numbers will be. A stated goal of the company is dealer health—making sure dealers sell more than they are forced to buy from the company is an important step to reach that goal.

Another important thing to look out for is the first numbers and commentary on how this year's new models perform. The first quarter is always especially important when it comes to sales of the new models.

As sequential financial numbers can be volatile based on shipment times, it's important to look out for any possible changes in guidance for the full year without reading too much into quarter-to-quarter metrics.

Besides the release of financial data from Q1 2026, we are even more excited about the company's strategic plan presentation. We are closely watching for any remarks regarding investments, cash burn, and plans to boost long-term demand for their motorcycles.

Risks and Conclusion

Even though the company is trading close to our adjusted tangible book values, there could still be some long-term downside for investors. Primarily, we see two main risks:

  1. Without a catalyst for re-rating, the stock could continue trading at or even below tangible book value for a long time, making opportunity risk a major concern for buyers.
  2. Investing in a turnaround or accelerated deterioration of fundamentals could lead to the company becoming cash-burning and destroying its tangible book value.

Investors should be able to better price both of these risks after digesting information from their upcoming strategic plan presentation.

The first risk could primarily be mitigated by the company continuing to push for aggressive share repurchases, forcing the stock to eventually trade at reasonable multiples above its tangible book value.

The second risk also depends on the company's plans going forward. A low-risk, low-reward scenario for investors certainly is managing the business for cash and continually right-sizing the company based on declining demand. A high-risk, high-reward scenario is the company investing heavily in a turnaround, spending money on new products and marketing.

If the company chooses to go down the high-risk path, there certainly is a substantial risk of the company investing its remaining capital in a failing turnaround, leaving the investors with even bigger losses.

data.title: Merck: Bullish Setup Into Q1 Earnings
data.description:
So, I am here again, with my analysis of Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK).

In my previous article, "Merck: Why Investors Should Remain Bullish Despite Patent Risks," I discussed why the subcutaneous version of pembrolizumab, called Keytruda Qlex, has a high chance of becoming Merck's next blockbuster, as well as the follow-up data from the Phase 2b KEYNOTE-942 trial, evaluating Moderna's (MRNA) intismeran autogene+Keytruda.

In this one, I'll focus on Merck's latest milestones, including its acquisition of Terns Pharmaceuticals (TERN) and the FDA approval of Idvynso on April 21, and, more importantly, what I expect from its Q1 earnings.

Terns' TERN-701 strengthens Merck's position in the chronic myeloid leukemia market

So, let me remind you that on March 25, it announced the acquisition of Terns for $5.7 billion.

And a month later, on April 24, it was informed about the end of the waiting period under the Hart-Scott-Rodino Act. Simply put, I believe Merck will close this deal before the end of May.

And in my view, TERN-701 is the "crown jewel" for which Merck paid billions of USD.

It is a highly selective allosteric inhibitor of the kinase activity of BCR-ABL1, specifically targeting the ABL1 myristoyl pocket.

Moreover, in preclinical studies, TERN-701 was more selective than Novartis' (NVS) Scemblix [asciminib, a first-in-class BCR-ABL1 STAMP inhibitor].

But still. Was this deal a smart strategic move for Merck?

I would say, yes.

First of all, on December 8 last year, Terns released updated results from the ongoing CARDINAL study [NCT06163430].

Namely, I want to talk about patients with chronic-phase CML [chronic myeloid leukemia] who were heavily pretreated with a median of 3 TKIs, and were taking TERN-701 at doses of 320 mg or 500 mg once daily.

So, in 30 efficacy-evaluable patients, the overall MMR [major molecular response] rate at 24 weeks was 80%. Moreover, at this recommended phase 2 dose of TERN-701, ≥ 320 mg QD, 36% of patients [10 out of 28] achieved deep molecular response [DMM].

And given these very strong numbers, coupled with its favorable safety profile, only strengthens my belief that TERN-701 will become the standard of care in 1L+ CML.

I said that for a reason.

In the Phase 3 ASCEMBL trial [NCT03106779], the major molecular response rate was only 25.5% in those taking asciminib at 24 weeks.

Also, the DMR rate was only 10.8% in the Scemblix arm.

But even these data, while weaker than those of TERN-701, are superior to other tyrosine kinase inhibitors, including Pfizer's (PFE) Bosulif and Bristol-Myers Squibb's (BMY) Sprycel.

And that's why, as I pointed out in the article "Novartis: Buy Thesis Fades Into Q1 2026 Results," sales of Scemblix grew 88.9% year-over-year to $391 million in Q4.

Thus, my first intermediate conclusion is that Merck was right to acquire Terns Pharmaceuticals.

I expect to see another solid performance from Keytruda and Winrevair

So, as you know, pembrolizumab is a PD-1 inhibitor and the flagship of Merck's oncology franchise.

In Q4, Keytruda+Keytruda Qlex generated about $8.37 billion in revenue for it, up 6.8% year-on-year.

Moreover, in the past 4 months, Merck has reached several milestones.

First of all, I highlight that the combo of Keytruda with Pfizer's Padcev reduced the risk of death by 35% versus neoadjuvant chemotherapy [P=0.0029] in cis-eligible patients with MIBC.

And also, Keytruda/Keytruda Qlex plus paclitaxel, with or without bevacizumab, got the EC's green light on April 2 for the treatment of certain patients suffering from platinum-resistant ovarian cancer.

To my great regret, WHO's Cancer Tomorrow doesn't provide numbers only for PD-L1-positive platinum-resistant ovarian cancer, but even without that, it's clear that the TAM is not so small, as about 45.8 thousand cases of ovarian cancer will be diagnosed only in the EU in 2050.

On the other hand, I was once again not disappointed with the "gem" of Merck's cardiovascular portfolio, Winrevair [sotatercept].

Alright. But why?

Winrevair net revenues rose 29.7% quarter-over-quarter to $467 million in Q4, in part due to its label expansion on October 27th last year.

And also, on March 29, in my view, Winrevair showed promising results in the Phase 2 CADENCE trial [NCT04945460].

In it, patients with Cpc-PH due to heart failure with preserved ejection fraction who took 0.3 mg/kg of sotatercept experienced a placebo-corrected reduction in pulmonary vascular resistance by 1.02 Wood units at week 24.

In my view, the results of the secondary endpoints further strengthen the evidence that Winrevair has direct cardiac and pulmonary vascular effects in these people with advanced HF.

And before I get to the "risks" section, I must note that under my "base case" scenario, the pembrolizumab franchise and Winrevair will generate $9.05 net revenues for Merck in Q1, up 20.9% YoY.

By the way, I also took into account that Roche's (RHHBY) Tecentriq, a key competitor of Keytruda in the PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint inhibitors market, once again showed weak performance.

In Q1 2026, its sales were only 811 million CHF, down 14.6% from last quarter.

Risks

While I have identified 7 risks that, in my view, could scare away income-oriented investors from adding Merck to their portfolios, the key one is the U.S. launch of generic versions of Janumet in May.

By the way, sales of this therapy for type 2 diabetes were $199 million in Q4, down 18.1% quarter-over-quarter and 22% YoY.

Takeaway

On April 30, Merck, one of the key players in the lung cancer and renal cell carcinoma therapeutics markets, will announce its first-quarter results.

According to SA’s data, Wall Street projects that its revenue will be $15.85 billion [from $15.23 billion to $16.32 billion; up 2% YoY] and non-GAAP EPS of -$1.47.

But personally, I'm a little bit more optimistic.

First of all, due to the dominant position of Merck’s Prevymis among the anti-CMV agents, Keytruda and its pneumococcal 21-valent vaccine called Capvaxive [its sales I expect to be $320 million vs. $279 million in Q4].

Well, my last remark is that I continue to cover Merck with a Buy rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Annaly: The Ice Cube That Still Pays Patient Investors
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Annaly Capital Management, Inc. (NLY) has earned its place in the high-yield, ice cube business model category. Its historical price decline and inability to recoup (at least fully) the lost Book Value "BV"/share is the primary reason. This trend is particularly notable given NLY's many accretive equity raises on its shares that typically trade at a premium to BV.

Someone might say this categorization is harsh, since it puts it on par with much riskier businesses I have covered and also categorized as having an ice-cube model, such as various BDCs (private credit companies whose tax laws limit their ability to build loan-loss reserves) and Oxford Lane Capital Corporation (OXLC), a wildcatter whose portfolio mostly consists of the equity portion of CLOs—the riskiest tranche in the CLO structure.

What sets NLY apart from its peers is the quality of its portfolio, consisting mostly of Mortgage-Backed Securities (MBS), whose principal and interest are guaranteed by Government-Sponsored Entities (GSEs), such as the Federal National Mortgage Association (FNMA).

This means that NLY's ice cube doesn't melt systematically but only occasionally and mainly because of exorbitant leverage that forces fire sales at adverse market conditions (resulting in capital losses) to avoid margin calls (as discussed below).

The cumulative return analysis below suggests that investors' returns improve with the length of the holding period. That's another feature that sets NLY apart from many of its peers in the ice-cube model category. All investors adopting a “buy and hold forever” strategy in the past ten years are sitting on positive total returns despite the BV/share and price/share declines. This hold rating balances NLY's high leverage and BV/share declines against the quality of its MBS portfolio.

Why NLY Needs Leverage to Work

NLY's business model requires leverage. For the sake of argument, let us imagine it without debt—a portfolio funded by common equity (shareholders' contributions). The portfolio size would be around $16.3 billion, roughly equal to the stockholders' equity as of March 2026. (For perspective, the actual portfolio size is $107 billion). As of Q1'26, the average yield on interest-earning assets stood at 5.35%. NLY is internally managed, and operating expenses knock off about 0.15% from revenue (based on management's operating expense target of 0.15% of assets, although the run rate figure was a bit higher in Q1'26). The point here is, even if the company distributes all of its income, a critical question remains. Would that 5.2% yield be enough to attract investors when they can buy a 30-year treasury note yielding 4.9%? Now, don't get me wrong, NLY's assets are of high quality (as discussed below), but delinquencies and defaults exist, some generating losses beyond the mortgage collateral. This 0.3% yield premium that NLY offers above the US Treasury isn't enough to make it attractive from a risk-adjusted viewpoint, alienating serious institutional investors. Without leverage, NLY is just another bond ETF at best and an inefficient vehicle to trade MBSs at worst. It needs leverage to boost yields and attract investors, particularly retail investors.

The Margin Borrowing Model

The quality of NLY's assets gives it access to a lot of borrowing. About $92 billion of its 107 billion portfolio is agency MBS, with GSEs guaranteeing interest and principal. Banks and lending institutions are happy to lend NLY as long as it posts its assets as collateral. In a leveraged scenario, the incremental ROE is the difference between what NLY earns on its MBS (and other assets) and the funding costs it incurs on this leverage. In Q1'26, average costs of funds stood at 3.93% (mostly repurchase agreements). With an average yield on interest-earning assets at 5.35%, the spread is 1.42%. This means that for each dollar of debt, net investment income (NII) increases by ~1.4 cents. That's a modest figure, especially given that it doesn't yet include operating expenses, which is why NLY borrows multiple times its equity, boosting economic leverage to 5.7x.

Since NLY's equity is the collateral for a debt multiple times larger, the REIT is essentially engaging in margin borrowing. Margin calls happen when the equity cushion becomes too small to cover losses in a much larger asset base.

The Math Behind BV/Share Decay

There are three forces that impact NLY's Book Value.

  1. Interest and investment income received from its portfolio (positive impact).
  2. Capital gains/losses on portfolio (mixed impact).
  3. Distributions (negative impact).

To understand BV/share movement, one needs to look at all these parts together. Book Value/Share could decrease even if capital gains are positive if the resulting Comprehensive Net Income is less than distributions.

Note: Comprehensive Income/Loss = Net Income/Loss + Capital Gains/Losses

Millions $ Net Income/Loss Other Comprehensive Income/Loss Comprehensive Income/Loss

2024 $1,012 $318 $1,165
2023 $(1,639) $2,374 $589
2022 $1,726 $(4,667) $(3,053)
2021 $2,396 $(2,416) $(134)

In fact, that is precisely what happened in the past ten years. Despite NLY generating positive Comprehensive Net Income, its Book Value/Share declined because the distributions were much larger.
